source: Learning bash with Rocky - First Script: Writing a first script, the shebang, and running it

questions.q: Which naming rules does the book give for a script?
options: Alphanumeric only (no spaces/accents), a .sh extension, and not the name of an existing command
It must start with a number
questions.why: Avoid existing command names, use only alphanumeric characters, and add the .sh extension.

options: The first line, starting with #! followed by the path to the interpreter
A comment anywhere in the file
The last line of the script
questions.why: The shebang is the first line: #! then the path to the command interpreter.

questions.q: Which shebang does the book recommend for a bash script, and why?
options: #!/usr/bin/env bash -- it increases portability (the interpreter is found wherever it lives)
questions.why: Using env locates the interpreter via PATH, so the script still works if bash lives elsewhere.

questions.q: Run the script hello-world.sh by passing it to the bash binary.
questions.why: bash <script> runs the file through the interpreter without needing execute permission.

questions.q: Give the owner execute permission on ./hello-world.sh.
questions.why: chmod u+x adds the execute right for the owner; you only do this once on a new script.

questions.q: After making it executable, how do you run a script that is in your current directory?
questions.why: You must prefix ./ (a path); the shell will not run a script in the current dir from a bare name.

questions.q: Why must you use ./ (or a full path) to run a script in the current directory?
options: The interpreter refuses a bare name unless the directory is in your PATH
questions.why: Without a path it is only found if placed in a PATH directory; otherwise use ./ or the full path.

questions.q: To use python instead of bash for a script, you change the shebang to...
questions.why: Swap the interpreter in the env shebang, e.g. #!/usr/bin/env ksh or #!/usr/bin/env python.
